facts_disclosed: Dropped the master's idea — not worth the time or money

turns.content: Quick question. If someone asks you what postgraduate qualifications are worth doing for a practising barrister, what would you say?

It depends on what the barrister is trying to achieve. An LLM or specialist master's can deepen expertise in a niche area, signal seriousness to solicitors or clients, and sometimes open doors to academic or policy work. But for someone already established in practice, the returns can be marginal — the time and cost need to justify themselves against what you'd gain from simply doing more high-quality cases. Are you considering something specific?

I was. Past tense. I'd been looking at the LLM in human rights law at UCL. Seemed like it might sharpen things up, give me an academic grounding in some of the policy questions I deal with daily. International criminal law modules looked interesting too.
UCL's programme does have a strong reputation, and the international criminal law strand is well regarded. What shifted it to past tense for you?

I spoke to three people who actually did it. Two barristers and one solicitor-advocate, all criminal practitioners. The consensus was unanimous and rather deflating. They said the academic content was fine but it didn't translate to practice in any meaningful way. One of them described it as 'an expensive year of reading things I could have read anyway.' The other said the networking was the only real value, and you can network without paying fifteen thousand pounds for the privilege.

That's telling feedback, especially coming from practitioners in your own field rather than academics. The gap between academic criminal law and the realities of defence practice can be significant. Did any of them feel it helped with career progression — tenancy applications, silk, judicial appointments, that sort of thing?

One thought it might have been a marginal factor in getting onto a Recorder panel, but she admitted she couldn't be sure. The other two said it made no difference whatsoever to their practice or their standing. And that was the nail in the coffin, really. I'm not interested in credentials for their own sake. If it doesn't make me a better advocate or open a door that's otherwise shut, it's dead. The whole idea is dead.

That sounds like a clear-headed decision. If three people who've been through it can't point to concrete benefits for someone in your position, the cost-benefit analysis speaks for itself. Fifteen thousand pounds and a year of evenings and weekends is a significant investment to make on a maybe.

Quite. And it's not just the money, it's the opportunity cost. That's a year I could spend building my practice, taking on more complex cases, developing a genuine specialism through actual work rather than coursework. I'd rather invest that time in something that moves the needle.

The opportunity cost point is well made. Practical experience in complex cases arguably builds expertise and reputation more effectively than academic credentials at your stage. Are you thinking about other ways to develop — perhaps focusing on a particular case type, or building a profile in a specific area?

I've been gravitating towards serious fraud and financial crime anyway. The cases are intellectually demanding, the preparation is intense, and frankly the fees are better which means I can continue to take legal aid work on the side without bankrupting myself. It feels like a more honest path than collecting letters after my name.

Financial crime is a strong area to develop in — it's growing, the cases tend to be substantial, and it rewards exactly the kind of detailed preparation and analytical rigour that seems to be your strength. You might consider whether any of the specialist training courses from the Fraud Lawyers Association or similar bodies would be useful. They're shorter, cheaper, and more practice-focused than a full master's.

Now that's a sensible suggestion. Short courses I can manage. Something I can do over a weekend or a few evenings without derailing everything else. I'll look into the FLA. Do you know if the SFO runs any practitioner workshops?

The SFO has historically run some engagement events and practitioner seminars, though they tend to be more about their processes and case approach than formal training. The Proceeds of Crime Lawyers Association (POCLA) also runs practical courses on confiscation and asset recovery that might be relevant if your fraud work touches on those areas. The Inns of Court sometimes host specialist CPD sessions too — worth checking what Gray's Inn or your own Inn has coming up.

Good. I'll have a look at what's available this autumn. Far more proportionate than committing to a full master's that three separate people have told me was a waste of their time. Right, I need to get back to a skeleton argument. Thank you.

Good luck with the skeleton. It sounds like you've made a well-reasoned decision — grounded in real evidence from people who've been through it rather than speculation. If you want help researching specific short courses or putting together a development plan for the fraud specialism, just let me know.
